chunk_summary: This section discusses interpreting anthropometric measures like MUAC, TSF, and height velocity in children with CKD or liver disease, emphasizing adjustments for edema, ascites, and growth standards.
learning_objectives: Understand the limitations of weight-based indices in children with CKD or liver disease due to fluid shifts.
Learn to use MUAC, TSF, and height velocity as reliable proxies for nutritional assessment in fluid-overloaded states.
Identify appropriate growth references for children under 5 years and older Indian children.
Recognize red flags in growth trends and their implications for nutritional interventions.
key_facts: Weight is unreliable in CKD with nephrotic edema or cirrhosis with ascites; use MUAC and TSF instead.
WHO growth standards are recommended for children under 5 years; Revised IAP charts are preferred for older Indian children.
Crossing down one major percentile channel over 3–6 months or a sustained fall in MUAC/TSF despite stable weight warrants intervention.
Height velocity and z-scores are critical for assessing chronic nutritional adequacy.
In infants, head circumference should be monitored due to risks of neurodevelopmental impairment from chronic undernutrition.
reference_quote: Weight-based indices can be misleading when extracellular fluid expands. In CKD, nephrotic-range edema or aggressive ultrafiltration can shift weight without changing muscle or fat; in cirrhosis, ascites and organomegaly can mask severe tissue loss.
document_key_topics: Anthropometric assessment in CKD and liver disease
Impact of edema and ascites on nutritional evaluation
